title: The 2019 Oscar Narratives, Explained | The Oscars Show (Ep. 129)
format: Sean and Amanda splice together a week of roundtables with Ringer colleagues: why A Star Is Born isn't the front-runner, next year's contenders, Hindsight Awards for this year's snubs, the Green Book/Bohemian Rhapsody outrage cycle, why Netflix wants an Oscar for Roma, and a full walk through preferential balloting.

segments.heading: Why Isn't A Star Is Born the Front-Runner?
segments.note: with Chris Ryan and Kate Halliwell

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: Eight nominations, second-most of the field, and once pitched as a five-for-five sweep of the Big Five. The hosts land on Bradley Cooper's guarded press campaign and a Best Director snub as the reasons it stalled; Kate pushes back that the ending landed fine for her.

segments.heading: Next Year's Contenders
segments.note: with Chris Ryan and Kate Halliwell

films.title: Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker
films.note: Chris's pick for a Best Picture nomination, a Return of the King-style thank-you note to the saga.

films.title: The Lion King
films.note: Jon Favreau's remake, floated as a popular-Oscar contender.

films.title: Toy Story 4
films.note: Past Toy Story films have flirted with Best Picture; whether this one does depends on a popular-Oscar category that may not happen.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig's follow-up to Lady Bird, starring Saoirse Ronan and Timothee Chalamet.

films.title: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood
films.note: Marielle Heller's Mr. Rogers biopic starring Tom Hanks, after Can You Ever Forgive Me.

films.title: The Farewell
films.note: Lulu Wang's Sundance breakout starring Awkwafina.

films.title: Ad Astra
films.note: James Gray's space opera with Brad Pitt; the hosts debate whether it's a Gravity or a cult curiosity the Academy skips.

films.title: Once Upon a Time in Hollywood
films.note: Tarantino's 10th film, with Brad Pitt, Leonardo DiCaprio, and Margot Robbie as Sharon Tate.

films.title: Avengers: Endgame
films.note: Floated half as a joke, including a Robert Downey Jr. Oscar campaign for his final Tony Stark turn.

films.title: The Report
films.note: Another Sundance title, starring Adam Driver and Annette Bening as Dianne Feinstein.

films.title: The Goldfinch
films.note: John Crowley's adaptation of the Donna Tartt novel, after Brooklyn, with an October release date that mirrors A Star Is Born's.

films.title: It Chapter Two
films.note: Andy Muschietti's sequel with Jessica Chastain and Bill Hader; horror rarely plays at the Oscars.

segments.heading: The Hindsight Awards
segments.note: the makeup calls the Oscars will owe in 10 or 20 years

picks.title: First Reformed
picks.note: Ethan Hawke's Best Actor award, a decade or so overdue.

picks.title: A Star Is Born
picks.note: Bradley Cooper's future Best Director award, for writing, playing, singing, and directing the thing and still getting shut out of the category.

picks.title: Mary Poppins Returns
picks.note: One half of Emily Blunt's future win, for a year in which she went unnominated for two very different lead performances.

picks.title: A Quiet Place
picks.note: The other half of Emily Blunt's future win.

picks.title: Black Panther
picks.note: Ryan Coogler's future Best Director award, for pulling off a Marvel movie that still feels entirely his own.

segments.heading: Green Book, Bohemian Rhapsody, and Oscar Outrage

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Won the Toronto audience award, keeps winning guild prizes, and has drawn real criticism over how it handles Nick Vallelonga's and Don Shirley's stories. Amanda notes a backlash-to-the-backlash among voters annoyed at being told not to like it.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: Trailed by Bryan Singer's exit and disputes over what it gets wrong about Freddie Mercury and Queen, but a box-office hit that keeps winning anyway.

films.title: Driving Miss Daisy
films.note: The 30-years-earlier version of the same white-driver, Black-passenger story the hosts say Green Book is still relitigating.

films.title: Three Billboards Outside Ebbing, Missouri
films.note: Last year's version of this same argument, over its redemption arc for a racist cop.

segments.heading: Why Does Netflix Want an Oscar?
segments.note: with Chris Ryan and Allison Herman

films.title: Roma
films.note: Alfonso Cuaron's odds-on Best Picture favorite; Netflix even paid to put it in theaters to keep voters comfortable with it.

films.title: Icarus
films.note: Netflix's prior Best Documentary Feature win.

films.title: The White Helmets
films.note: Netflix's Best Documentary Short win from a few years back.

films.title: Mudbound
films.note: Netflix's first serious Best Picture push; it didn't get the nomination.

films.title: To All the Boys I've Loved Before
films.note: Cited as the kind of purely commercial hit Netflix will keep making alongside its prestige bids.

films.title: Bird Box
films.note: Same point: a hit with no awards ambitions.

films.title: The Ballad of Buster Scruggs
films.note: The Coen brothers' anthology, which picked up three nominations despite not being built as an awards play.

films.title: Velvet Buzzsaw
films.note: The Jake Gyllenhaal/Dan Gilroy movie released a few weeks after Roma's push, cited as proof Netflix has more prestige-adjacent titles banked for next fall.

films.title: High Flying Bird
films.note: The Steven Soderbergh movie released the same window, same point about Netflix's bench.

segments.heading: Predicting Best Picture
segments.note: a walk through preferential balloting, film by film, least to most likely

films.title: Vice
films.note: Broad, across-the-board nominations but polarizing reception and few guild wins; picked as least likely to win.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: A dark-horse Best Director case for Spike Lee, but the excitement is about him more than the film's Best Picture chances.

films.title: A Star Is Born
films.note: No Best Director or Best Editing nomination, the two things that usually travel with a Best Picture win, though its box office and awards-season press push give it a late, unlikely case.

films.title: The Favourite
films.note: Tied for the most nominations, and the projected Best Original Screenplay winner, which the hosts read as a sign it's everyone's well-liked third choice rather than anyone's first.

films.title: Black Panther
films.note: Its SAG Best Ensemble win is the whole case; no directing or acting nominations undercut it.

films.title: Bohemian Rhapsody
films.note: Went from a zero-chance punchline to a real threat after its Golden Globes win, on the strength of being a crowd-pleasing hit with a Best Actor front-runner in Rami Malek.

films.title: Green Book
films.note: Not the box-office hit Bohemian Rhapsody is, but ahead in guild wins, with a dedicated bloc of voters ranking it first or second.

films.title: Roma
films.note: The favorite, and an unusual one: black-and-white, foreign-language, from Netflix, with Cuaron all but locked for Best Director. The worry is that it collects second-place votes without enough firsts.

films: The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King
